toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""
extract_attributes.py

Extracts data attributes from the AnaCredit guidelines PDF (chapters 4 and 5)
and writes them to a CSV file.

Key design decisions:
- Single-pass flat line scan over pages 57-145 (0-indexed 56-144).
- Section headers detected by accumulating consecutive bold+size>=11 lines.
- "Data attribute(s):" labels detected, including the edge case where the
  label is split across two lines ("Data" / "attribute:") on page 68.
- Attribute names are bold lines (size 9-11) that immediately follow a label.
- Description text accumulated until next attribute label.
- Superscript footnote numbers (size<7, bold) and footnote body blocks
  (bottom 15% of page, all text size<=8.5) are filtered out.
"""

import fitz  # PyMuPDF
import csv
import re
import os

PDF_PATH = "/home/perni/clexp/AnaCredit/docs/anacredit-guidelines-data.pdf"
CSV_PATH = "/home/perni/clexp/AnaCredit/anacredit_data_attributes.csv"

FIRST_PAGE = 56   # 0-indexed; PDF page 57
LAST_PAGE  = 144  # 0-indexed; PDF page 145 (inclusive)

SECTION_PATTERNS = [
    (re.compile(r"4\..*counterparty reference data",              re.I | re.S),
     "Counterparty reference data"),
    (re.compile(r"5\.1\s+Instrument dataset",                     re.I),
     "Instrument dataset"),
    (re.compile(r"5\.2\s+Financial dataset",                      re.I),
     "Financial dataset"),
    (re.compile(r"5\.3\s+Counterparty.instrument dataset",        re.I),
     "Counterparty-instrument dataset"),
    (re.compile(r"5\.4\s+Joint liabilities dataset",              re.I),
     "Joint liabilities dataset"),
    (re.compile(r"5\.5\s+Accounting dataset",                     re.I),
     "Accounting dataset"),
    (re.compile(r"5\.6\s+Protection received dataset",            re.I),
     "Protection received dataset"),
    (re.compile(r"5\.7\s+Instrument.protection received dataset",  re.I),
     "Instrument-protection received dataset"),
    (re.compile(r"5\.8\s+Counterparty risk dataset",              re.I),
     "Counterparty risk dataset"),
    (re.compile(r"5\.9\s+Counterparty default dataset",           re.I),
     "Counterparty default dataset"),
    (re.compile(r"5\.10\s+Protection provider dataset",           re.I),
     "Protection provider dataset"),
]


def match_section(text):
    t = " ".join(text.split())
    for pattern, name in SECTION_PATTERNS:
        if pattern.search(t):
            return name
    return None


def is_superscript(span):
    return span["size"] < 7.0 and bool(span["flags"] & 1)


def is_footnote_body_block(block, page_height):
    if block["bbox"][1] <= page_height * 0.85:
        return False
    sizes = [s["size"] for ln in block["lines"] for s in ln["spans"] if s["text"].strip()]
    return bool(sizes) and max(sizes) <= 8.5


def collect_all_lines(doc):
    """
    Collect all text lines from the target page range as a flat list of dicts:
      text     : cleaned line text (all spans joined)
      bold     : True if all non-empty spans are bold (flags & 16)
      max_size : maximum font size among spans
      pg       : 1-indexed page number (for debugging)
    """
    lines = []
    for pg_idx in range(FIRST_PAGE, LAST_PAGE + 1):
        page = doc[pg_idx]
        ph = page.rect.height
        blocks = page.get_text("dict", flags=fitz.TEXT_PRESERVE_WHITESPACE)["blocks"]
        for block in blocks:
            if block["type"] != 0 or is_footnote_body_block(block, ph):
                continue
            for line in block["lines"]:
                spans = [s for s in line["spans"]
                         if s["text"].strip() and not is_superscript(s)]
                if not spans:
                    continue
                text = " ".join(s["text"].strip() for s in spans).strip()
                if not text or re.match(r"^Page\s+\d+\s*$", text):
                    continue
                lines.append({
                    "text":     text,
                    "bold":     all(bool(s["flags"] & 16) for s in spans),
                    "max_size": max(s["size"] for s in spans),
                    "pg":       pg_idx + 1,
                })
    return lines


# Patterns to detect "Data attribute(s):" labels (full and split variants)
LABEL_FULL  = re.compile(r"^Data attributes?\s*:\s*$", re.I)
LABEL_PART1 = re.compile(r"^Data\s*$", re.I)
LABEL_PART2 = re.compile(r"^attributes?\s*:\s*$", re.I)


def run_state_machine(lines):
    records = []

    dataset        = None
    attr           = None
    desc           = []
    expecting_name = False   # True after seeing a "Data attribute(s):" label
    hdr_buf        = []      # accumulate consecutive bold+size>=11 lines for section header detection
    prev_was_data  = False   # for split "Data" / "attribute:" handling

    def flush():
        nonlocal attr, desc
        if attr and dataset:
            text = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", " ".join(desc)).strip()
            records.append({"data_set": dataset, "data_attribute": attr,
                             "description": text})
        attr = None
        desc = []

    def try_match_hdr():
        """Try to match hdr_buf as a known section header. Return matched name or None."""
        nonlocal dataset, expecting_name
        combined = " ".join(hdr_buf)
        name = match_section(combined)
        if name:
            flush()
            dataset = name
            expecting_name = False
            return name
        return None

    i = 0
    while i < len(lines):
        ln       = lines[i]
        text     = ln["text"]
        bold     = ln["bold"]
        max_size = ln["max_size"]

        # ── Section header accumulation ──────────────────────────────────────
        # Section headers are bold AND size >= 11 (e.g. "5.5", "Accounting dataset")
        if bold and max_size >= 11.0:
            hdr_buf.append(text)
            matched = try_match_hdr()
            if matched:
                hdr_buf.clear()
                prev_was_data = False
            # else keep accumulating
            i += 1
            continue

        # ── Flush pending header buffer if current line is NOT a header ───────
        if hdr_buf:
            matched = try_match_hdr()
            if matched:
                hdr_buf.clear()
            else:
                # Not a section header — the buffered bold lines are body/subheads
                # Add them to description if we have a current attribute
                for bt in hdr_buf:
                    if attr:
                        desc.append(bt)
                hdr_buf.clear()
            prev_was_data = False

        # ── Handle split "Data" / "attribute:" label (page 68 edge case) ─────
        if LABEL_PART1.match(text):
            prev_was_data = True
            i += 1
            continue

        if prev_was_data and LABEL_PART2.match(text):
            # Reconstruct the full label
            expecting_name = True
            prev_was_data  = False
            i += 1
            continue

        prev_was_data = False

        # ── Full "Data attribute(s):" label ──────────────────────────────────
        if LABEL_FULL.match(text):
            expecting_name = True
            i += 1
            continue

        # ── Attribute name: bold line at size 9-11 following a label ─────────
        # The name may wrap across multiple bold lines (e.g. "Fair value changes
        # due to changes in credit risk before" / "purchase").
        if expecting_name and bold and 9.0 <= max_size <= 11.0:
            flush()
            name_parts = [text]
            # Greedily consume subsequent bold ~10pt lines as part of the name,
            # as long as they are NOT themselves "Data attribute:" labels.
            j = i + 1
            while j < len(lines):
                nxt = lines[j]
                if (nxt["bold"] and 9.0 <= nxt["max_size"] <= 11.0
                        and not LABEL_FULL.match(nxt["text"])
                        and not LABEL_PART1.match(nxt["text"])):
                    name_parts.append(nxt["text"])
                    j += 1
                else:
                    break
            attr = " ".join(name_parts)
            desc = []
            expecting_name = False
            i = j
            continue

        # ── Body text ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
        expecting_name = False
        if attr:
            desc.append(text)

        i += 1

    flush()
    return records


def write_csv(records, path):
    os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(path), exist_ok=True)
    with open(path, "w", newline="", encoding="utf-8-sig") as f:
        writer = csv.DictWriter(f,
                                fieldnames=["data_set", "data_attribute", "description"],
                                delimiter=";",
                                quoting=csv.QUOTE_ALL)
        writer.writeheader()
        writer.writerows(records)
    print(f"Written {len(records)} rows to {path}")



if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("Extracting data attributes from AnaCredit PDF...\n")
    doc = fitz.open(PDF_PATH)
    lines = collect_all_lines(doc)
    doc.close()
    print(f"Collected {len(lines)} text lines from pages {FIRST_PAGE+1}-{LAST_PAGE+1}.\n")

    records = run_state_machine(lines)

    from collections import Counter
    counts = Counter(r["data_set"] for r in records)
    print("Attributes per dataset:")
    total = 0
    for _, name in SECTION_PATTERNS:
        n = counts.get(name, 0)
        total += n
        print(f"  {n:3d}  {name}")
    print(f"  ---\n  {total:3d}  TOTAL\n")

    write_csv(records, CSV_PATH)
    write_xlsx(records, XLSX_PATH)

    print("\n--- First 20 rows ---")
    print(f"{'data_set':<45} {'data_attribute':<45} {'description (first 80 chars)'}")
    print("-" * 140)
    for r in records[:20]:
        print(f"{r['data_set']:<45} {r['data_attribute']:<45} {r['description'][:80]}")

---
name: AnaCredit skill
description: Context and approach for AnaCredit data attribute extraction from ECB guidelines PDF
type: project
---

AnaCredit is a European Central Bank (ECB) credit register reporting framework. The user works with AnaCredit guidelines in a banking context.

**Key files:**
- PDF: `/home/perni/clexp/AnaCredit/docs/anacredit-guidelines-data.pdf` — ECB AnaCredit reporting guidelines
- Excel: `/home/perni/clexp/AnaCredit/docs/anacredit-codelist-version-2-8-data.xlsx` — reference codelist (use instead of any URLs in the PDF)
- Extraction script: `/home/perni/clexp/AnaCredit/src/extract_attributes.py`
- Output CSV: `/home/perni/clexp/AnaCredit/anacredit_data_attributes.csv`

**CSV structure:** semicolon-delimited, columns: `data_set`, `data_attribute`, `description`

**Extracted datasets (120 attributes total):**
- Chapter 4: Counterparty reference data (29 attributes)
- Chapter 5: Instrument dataset (25), Financial dataset (13), Counterparty-instrument dataset (5), Joint liabilities dataset (5), Accounting dataset (18), Protection received dataset (10), Instrument-protection received dataset (5), Counterparty risk dataset (3), Counterparty default dataset (4), Protection provider dataset (3)

**Why:** Extraction uses PyMuPDF (fitz), single-pass state machine scanning pages 57-145 of the PDF. Data attributes are identified by "Data attribute:" headers; descriptions run until the next attribute or dataset boundary.

**How to apply:** When user asks about AnaCredit data attributes, datasets, or extending this work — refer to the existing script and CSV as the base. The codelist Excel is the authoritative reference for coded values (not URLs in the PDF).

input.content:
---
name: AnaCredit skill
description: Context, files, and approach for AnaCredit data extraction and validation (ECB credit register)
type: project
---

AnaCredit is a European Central Bank (ECB) credit register reporting framework. The user works with AnaCredit guidelines in a banking context (Deutsche Bundesbank).

## Source documents
- `/home/perni/clexp/AnaCredit/docs/anacredit-guidelines-data.pdf` — ECB AnaCredit reporting guidelines (English), chapters 4-5 cover datasets and data attributes
- `/home/perni/clexp/AnaCredit/docs/anacredit-codelist-version-2-8-data.xlsx` — authoritative reference for coded values; use this instead of any URLs in the PDF
- `/home/perni/clexp/AnaCredit/docs/anacredit-handbuch-validierungsregeln-version-22-data.pdf` — Deutsche Bundesbank validation rules handbook (German), v22 valid from 01.08.2026; Section 4.2 covers counterparty completeness rules (CY0010–CY0220), Section 4.4 covers consistency rules (CN_ prefix)

## Scripts
- `/home/perni/clexp/AnaCredit/src/extract_attributes.py` — extracts all data attributes from guidelines PDF to CSV; uses PyMuPDF (fitz), single-pass state machine, pages 57-145
- `/home/perni/clexp/AnaCredit/src/validate_counterparty.py` — validates counterparty CSV data against validation rules; loads legal form codes from codelist Excel at startup

## Output files
- `/home/perni/clexp/AnaCredit/anacredit_data_attributes.csv` — 120 attributes across 11 datasets; semicolon-delimited, utf-8-sig encoding
- `/home/perni/clexp/AnaCredit/sample_counterparty.csv` — 5 sample counterparty records for testing the validator

## CSV conventions (confirmed preferences)
- Separator: semicolon (`;`)
- Encoding: `utf-8-sig` (UTF-8 with BOM) — preserves ü, ä, ö and other international characters
- No Excel output — CSV only

## Extracted datasets (120 attributes total)
- Chapter 4: Counterparty reference data (29)
- Chapter 5: Instrument dataset (25), Financial dataset (13), Counterparty-instrument dataset (5), Joint liabilities dataset (5), Accounting dataset (18), Protection received dataset (10), Instrument-protection received dataset (5), Counterparty risk dataset (3), Counterparty default dataset (4), Protection provider dataset (3)

## Validator details
- Implements completeness rules CY0010–CY0220 and consistency rules (CN_ prefix) for counterparty reference data
- Loads 1,087 legal form codes from codelist Excel; zero runtime internet dependencies
- CLI: `python3 validate_counterparty.py <input.csv> [--output results.csv] [--no-warnings] [--summary]`
- Exit code 1 if any ERRORs found (pipeline-friendly)

## How to apply
- When extending validation to other datasets (instrument, financial, etc.) — follow the same pattern as validate_counterparty.py, referencing the relevant section of the validation rules handbook
- The codelist Excel is always the authoritative source for allowed coded values
- All new CSVs: semicolon separator, utf-8-sig encoding

content.text:
Two things need syncing — the **project files** and the **Claude memory/settings**:

**1. Project files (`/home/perni/clexp`)**
Initialize as a git repo and push to a private GitHub/GitLab. This covers all scripts, CLAUDE.md, and output files automatically.
```bash
cd /home/perni/clexp
git init
git add .
git commit -m "initial"
# push to private repo
```

**2. Claude memory + settings (`~/.claude/`)**
This is outside the project — it holds your memories and settings. Two options:

**Option A — include memories in the clexp repo** (simplest):
Add a setup script that copies/symlinks the memory folder into place on the second laptop. Clean, auditable, travels with the project.

**Option B — dotfiles repo for `~/.claude/`**:
A separate private git repo just for `~/.claude/settings.json`, `~/.claude/keybindings.json`, and the `projects/` memory folder. More portable if you work across multiple projects.

---

**My recommendation: Option A**

Since your memory is project-specific (path contains `-home-perni-clexp`), keep it with the project. Add a `setup.sh` at the root that bootstraps the memory on a new machine:

```bash
# setup.sh — run once on a new machine
MEMORY_DST="$HOME/.claude/projects/-home-perni-clexp/memory"
mkdir -p "$MEMORY_DST"
rsync -av "$(dirname "$0")/.claude-memory/" "$MEMORY_DST/"
```

And store the memory files under `clexp/.claude-memory/` in git.

Want me to set this up?

message.content: is it possible to do this all via git pull and git update  ?

content.text:
Yes — cleanest approach is two repos:

**Repo 1: `clexp` (project)**
Already your working directory. Just `git init` + push. On the second laptop: `git clone` then `git pull` to stay in sync.

**Repo 2: `dotfiles` (Claude settings + memory)**
A private repo for `~/.claude/`. On both machines, clone it to `~/.claude/` — then `git pull` on either machine picks up new memories and settings automatically.

```
~/.claude/
├── settings.json          ← synced
├── keybindings.json       ← synced  
└── projects/
    └── -home-perni-clexp/
        └── memory/        ← synced (your learnings)
```

**Workflow:**
```bash
# After a session on laptop 1:
cd ~/.claude && git add -A && git commit -m "update memories" && git push

# On laptop 2, before starting:
cd ~/.claude && git pull
cd ~/clexp && git pull
```

**One caveat:** the memory path `-home-perni-clexp` is derived from the absolute path `/home/perni/clexp`. As long as both laptops use the same WSL username (`perni`) and same project path, it works transparently — no setup script needed.

Want me to create a `.gitignore` for the `~/.claude/` repo (there are cache files and session logs you won't want to commit)?

#!/bin/bash
# Sync working directories into containers repo, commit and push.
# Run before leaving a laptop; do git pull on the other laptop to resume.

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"

    --exclude '__pycache__/' \
    --exclude '*.pyc' \
    --exclude '.DS_Store' \
    ../clexp/AnaCredit/ "$SCRIPT_DIR/AnaCredit/"

    --exclude 'statsig/' \
    --exclude 'cache/' \

git add .
git commit
REMOTE_URL="github.com/$GIT_USER/containers.git"

#!/bin/bash
# https://www.datacamp.com/tutorial/set-up-and-configure-mysql-in-docker
# Add all files to be tracked
cp -r ../clexp/experiments/* experiments
git add .
# commit will open nano for message 
git commit 
# cpnfigure a remote # the URL is without https i.e. userid needs to be provied with token
REMOTE_URL="github.com/$GIT_USER/containers.git"
# push to a remote repository  GIT_ISER and token are set before calling this 
git push -u https://$GIT_USER:$GIT_ACCESS_TOKEN@$REMOTE_URL main
